title: Data Foundation
text: Engineering POV

title: Data Foundation
text:
Why this page exists
Most portfolio sites show charts and code, but quietly assume the data fits in a CSV. That worked in 2015. It doesn’t reflect how modern analytics teams actually operate. When you’re analyzing 50GB of Wikipedia traffic or years of FEC filings, your first decision isn’t “which chart library?” — it’s “how do I store this so I don’t reload it from scratch every time?”
This page documents the engineering choices behind the case studies. They’re not glamorous. They’re the foundation everything else sits on.

title: Data Foundation
text:
The decision tree
For each dataset, I work through the same sequence:



1


What’s the access pattern?
Is this data read once and discarded, or queried repeatedly? Are queries time-bounded (last 30 days), entity-bounded (one client), or full-table scans? The right answer shapes everything downstream.




2


What’s the natural partition key?
Almost always date or time. Sometimes a categorical (DMA, client_id). Partitioning by the column you filter on most reduces query cost by orders of magnitude.




3


What’s the right format?
CSV for tiny exploratory data. Parquet for anything you’ll query more than twice. JSON only when the schema is genuinely heterogeneous. Never, ever Excel as a system of record.




4


What’s the right query engine?
DuckDB for ad-hoc SQL on local Parquet. Polars for transformations. Athena/BigQuery for cloud-scale. Pandas only if the data is small enough that it doesn’t matter.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Why Parquet, specifically
Three reasons that compound:
Columnar storage means column-selective queries are cheap. When you read a CSV with 50 columns and only need 3, you’re still parsing all 50. Parquet stores each column contiguously, so reading 3 columns is genuinely 6% of the work, not 100%.
Compression is dramatic and free. Real numbers from the case studies on this site:









Dataset
CSV size
Parquet size
Ratio




Wikipedia hourly pageviews (1 month)
6.2 GB
412 MB
15× smaller


FEC operating expenditures (2024 cycle)
1.4 GB
96 MB
14× smaller


CMS Open Payments (general payments, 1 yr)
2.1 GB
178 MB
12× smaller



That’s not just disk — it’s network transfer time, S3 storage cost, and memory pressure during reads. All compounding.
Schema is part of the file. No more “wait, is date a string or a timestamp?” The Parquet file knows. Pandas, Polars, DuckDB, Athena, Spark — they all read the same file the same way. No parsing surprises.
The cost: Parquet is binary. You can’t cat it or open it in a text editor. That’s a fair trade for everything you get back.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Partitioning strategy
This is where most teams get it wrong. The instinct is to partition by everything possible (“partition by year, month, day, hour, dma, channel…”). The reality: over-partitioning is worse than under-partitioning because the metadata overhead destroys query performance.
The rule I use:

Partition by what you filter on. Don’t partition by what you only group by.
The single most useful partitioning heuristic.

For time-series data: partition by date. That’s it. Most queries are “last N days” or “this month” — date partitioning makes those skip entire directories of files. Adding hour-level partitioning means thousands of tiny files, each with overhead. Not worth it unless you genuinely run hour-bounded queries.
For mixed dimensions: partition by the high-cardinality column you filter on. For ad-tech data with client_id, partition by client. For multi-tenant data with region, partition by region. Combine with date if the volume justifies it.
The directory structure ends up looking like:
data/
├── wikipedia_pageviews/
│   ├── date=2024-01-01/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   ├── date=2024-01-02/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   └── ...
├── fec_expenditures/
│   ├── cycle=2022/
│   │   ├── month=01/
│   │   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   │   └── month=02/...
│   └── cycle=2024/...
└── cms_payments/
    ├── year=2023/
    │   └── part-0.parquet
    └── year=2024/
        └── part-0.parquet
This is Hive-style partitioning — the directory names encode the partition values. Every modern engine (DuckDB, Polars, Athena, Spark) auto-detects this layout.

title: Data Foundation
text:
The query layer
For ad-hoc analysis: DuckDB on Parquet is the most underrated combination in modern analytics. You get full SQL, partition pruning, column pruning, and zero infrastructure. It runs on a laptop and queries 50GB of Parquet faster than most cloud warehouses query a table that size.
import duckdb

# Query directly against Parquet — no loading step
result = duckdb.sql("""
    SELECT
        date,
        SUM(views) AS total_views,
        COUNT(DISTINCT article) AS articles
    FROM 'data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet'
    WHERE date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
      AND project = 'en.wikipedia'
    GROUP BY date
    ORDER BY date
""").df()
The WHERE date BETWEEN ... clause causes DuckDB to skip every partition outside that range — it never even opens those files. Same query against the equivalent CSV would take 50–100× longer.
For transformations: Polars. Same lazy-evaluation model as Spark, single-machine execution, written in Rust, ~5–10× faster than pandas on real workloads. The API is more disciplined than pandas (which I view as a feature, not a bug).
import polars as pl

df = (
    pl.scan_parquet('data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet')
    .filter(pl.col('project') == 'en.wikipedia')
    .filter(pl.col('date').is_between('2024-01-01', '2024-01-31'))
    .group_by('date')
    .agg([
        pl.col('views').sum().alias('total_views'),
        pl.col('article').n_unique().alias('articles'),
    ])
    .sort('date')
    .collect()
)
scan_parquet is the lazy version — Polars builds an execution plan, applies predicate pushdown to the Parquet files (so only matching row-groups are read), and only materializes the result when .collect() is called.

title: Data Foundation
section: What this signals to a hiring manager
text:
What this signals to a hiring manager
I’m direct about why this page exists. For a Manager or Director-level analytics role, the question isn’t “can you write a SQL query?” It’s “do you make decisions like an engineer when the situation calls for it?”
Showing the storage layer signals three things:

I think in production terms. Cost, latency, and reproducibility matter, not just the final chart.
I make tool choices deliberately. Polars, DuckDB, Parquet aren’t on this page because they’re trendy. They’re here because they’re the right choice for the access patterns I described.
I can lead a team that operates at this level. A team that ships code-driven reports needs an architecture this team can stand on. That architecture is an analytics leadership responsibility, not a “let the data engineering team handle it” handoff.

title: Data Foundation
text:
Honest trade-offs
I want to be clear about what this approach isn’t good for:

Real-time / streaming. Parquet is batch. If you need sub-minute freshness, this is the wrong layer. (You’d add Kafka + a streaming engine on top.)
Frequent row-level updates. Parquet files are immutable; updating individual rows means rewriting partitions. For mostly-append-only data (which describes most analytics workloads), this is fine.
Tiny datasets. If your data fits comfortably in a CSV that opens in Excel, just use a CSV. The infrastructure overhead isn’t justified.
Cross-org data sharing. Parquet works in any modern engine, but if your stakeholders open everything in Excel, you’ll still need to export to xlsx for delivery. (Which is fine — generate it from the Parquet source.)

The point isn’t that Parquet/Polars/DuckDB is universally correct. The point is that making the choice deliberately, and being able to explain why, is what separates senior analytics work from junior analytics work.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text: Reporting Engineering · End-to-End Pipeline

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
The Business Problem
Recurring reports are where analytics teams quietly hemorrhage hours. A monthly client deliverable that takes 6 hours to assemble, times 12 months, times multiple clients, adds up to enormous opportunity cost.
The CMS Open Payments dataset is a useful proving ground because it has the qualities that make real client work hard:

Real volume (millions of records per year)
Real messiness (free-text purpose fields, inconsistent vendor naming, missing values)
Real reporting needs (companies and analysts care about this — there’s a publication audience)

The goal: a single command produces a publication-quality monthly summary report (HTML and Excel) for any pharmaceutical company in the dataset, ready to send to a stakeholder.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
The Approach



1


Modular cleaning functions
Cleaning logic lives in standalone R functions, not in the report itself. Each function does one thing and is independently testable.




2


Parameterized Quarto report
The report (monthly-summary.qmd) takes parameters: company name, reporting period. One template renders for any company.




3


Publication-quality tables (gt)
Use gt and gtExtras for tables that look like they belong in a Wall Street Journal feature, not a Jupyter notebook.




4


Excel output via openxlsx2
Same data, formatted Excel workbook with frozen panes, conditional formatting, and multiple sheets — for stakeholders who live in Excel.

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: Step 1: Load CMS Open Payments data
text:
Step 1: Load CMS Open Payments data
The fetch script (data/fetch_cms.py) downloads the General Payments bulk file from download.cms.gov/openpayments/, filters it to the eight target manufacturers using a Polars lazy scan, and writes the result to Parquet. CMS rotates the bulk filename on every refresh, so the script accepts the current URL as --url — see the README for instructions.
If the cache is absent (e.g. you cloned the repo and haven’t fetched yet), the chunk falls back to a clearly-labeled synthetic dataset that mirrors the real schema, so the rest of the case study still renders. The synthetic path is only there as a fallback — the goal is real data.


Load CMS Open Payments — real if cached, synthetic fallback otherwise
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(stringr)

cache_path <- file.path("..", "data", "cache", "cms", "general_payments.parquet")
using_real_data <- file.exists(cache_path)

if (using_real_data) {
  payments_raw <- read_parquet(cache_path) %>%
    rename(
      applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name = company,
      physician_specialty                                            = specialty,
      recipient_state                                                = state,
      date_of_payment                                                = payment_date,
      nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value                         = payment_type,
      total_amount_of_payment_usdollars                              = amount_usd
    )
  cat("Source: REAL CMS Open Payments (filtered to 8 manufacturers via fetch_cms.py)\n")
} else {
  cat("Source: SYNTHETIC fallback (CMS cache not found at ", cache_path, ")\n", sep = "")
  cat("  → Run `python data/fetch_cms.py --url <current-bulk-url>` for real data.\n\n")
  set.seed(2024)
  n_records <- 8000
  companies <- c(
    "Pfizer Inc.", "Johnson & Johnson", "Merck & Co.",
    "AbbVie Inc.", "Eli Lilly and Company", "Bristol-Myers Squibb",
    "Novartis Pharmaceuticals", "GlaxoSmithKline LLC"
  )
  specialties <- c(
    "Internal Medicine", "Cardiology", "Oncology", "Family Medicine",
    "Psychiatry", "Neurology", "Endocrinology", "Dermatology",
    "Orthopedic Surgery", "Anesthesiology"
  )
  states <- c("CA","TX","NY","FL","IL","PA","OH","GA","NC","MI",
              "NJ","VA","WA","AZ","MA","TN","IN","MO","MD","WI")
  payment_types <- c(
    "Compensation for services other than consulting" = 0.12,
    "Consulting Fee" = 0.18, "Food and Beverage" = 0.32,
    "Travel and Lodging" = 0.10, "Education" = 0.08,
    "Honoraria" = 0.06, "Grant" = 0.04, "Royalty or License" = 0.02,
    "Charitable Contribution" = 0.03, "Gift" = 0.05
  )
  payments_raw <- tibble(
    record_id = seq_len(n_records),
    applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name = sample(
      companies, n_records, replace = TRUE,
      prob = c(0.18, 0.16, 0.14, 0.12, 0.11, 0.10, 0.10, 0.09)
    ),
    physician_specialty = sample(specialties, n_records, replace = TRUE),
    recipient_state = sample(states, n_records, replace = TRUE),
    date_of_payment = sample(
      seq.Date(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-31"), by = "day"),
      n_records, replace = TRUE
    ),
    nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value = sample(
      names(payment_types), n_records, replace = TRUE,
      prob = unname(payment_types)
    )
  ) %>%
    mutate(
      total_amount_of_payment_usdollars = round(rlnorm(n(), meanlog = 4.8, sdlog = 1.6), 2),
      recipient_state = ifelse(runif(n()) < 0.015, NA_character_, recipient_state),
      physician_specialty = ifelse(runif(n()) < 0.01, NA_character_, physician_specialty)
    )
  dupe_rows <- payments_raw %>% slice_sample(n = 150)
  payments_raw <- bind_rows(payments_raw, dupe_rows)
}

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Step 2: Modular cleaning functions
The cleaning lives in standalone functions, in a separate R/ directory in production. Each function is testable and reusable.


Modular cleaning functions (would normally live in R/clean.R)
#' Standardize CMS column names
#' @param df Raw CMS payments tibble
clean_column_names <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    rename(
      company       = applicable_manufacturer_or_applicable_gpo_making_payment_name,
      specialty     = physician_specialty,
      state         = recipient_state,
      payment_date  = date_of_payment,
      payment_type  = nature_of_payment_or_transfer_of_value,
      amount_usd    = total_amount_of_payment_usdollars
    )
}

#' Remove duplicate filings (keeping first occurrence)
deduplicate_records <- function(df) {
  df %>% distinct(record_id, .keep_all = TRUE)
}

#' Handle missing values with documented defaults
handle_missing <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      state = if_else(is.na(state), "Unknown", state),
      specialty = if_else(is.na(specialty), "Not Reported", specialty)
    )
}

#' Flag outliers using IQR method (don't remove, just flag for review)
flag_outliers <- function(df, multiplier = 3) {
  df %>%
    group_by(payment_type) %>%
    mutate(
      .q1 = quantile(amount_usd, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      .q3 = quantile(amount_usd, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      .iqr = .q3 - .q1,
      is_outlier = amount_usd > .q3 + multiplier * .iqr
    ) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    select(-.q1, -.q3, -.iqr)
}

#' Add derived time dimensions
add_time_dimensions <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      year = year(payment_date),
      quarter = paste0("Q", quarter(payment_date)),
      month = month(payment_date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
      year_month = floor_date(payment_date, "month")
    )
}

#' Full cleaning pipeline
clean_pipeline <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    clean_column_names() %>%
    deduplicate_records() %>%
    handle_missing() %>%
    flag_outliers() %>%
    add_time_dimensions()
}

# Apply
payments <- clean_pipeline(payments_raw)

cat("After cleaning:\n")


After cleaning:

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Step 3: Publication-quality tables with gt
This is where R earns its keep. gt and gtExtras produce tables that genuinely belong in a published report.


Build publication-quality summary table with gt
library(gt)
library(scales)

# Pick the top-spend manufacturer in the cleaned data as the parameterized example.
# Real CMS company names vary slightly across program years (e.g. "Pfizer, Inc." vs
# "PFIZER INC."), so we resolve at run time rather than hard-coding a string.
target_company <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier) %>%
  group_by(company) %>%
  summarise(spend = sum(amount_usd, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(spend)) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  pull(company)

company_summary <- payments %>%
  filter(company == target_company, !is_outlier) %>%
  group_by(payment_type) %>%
  summarise(
    n_payments = n(),
    total_amount = sum(amount_usd),
    median_amount = median(amount_usd),
    avg_amount = mean(amount_usd),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(total_amount)) %>%
  mutate(
    pct_of_total = total_amount / sum(total_amount)
  )

summary_year <- year(min(payments$payment_date, na.rm = TRUE))
company_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = md(paste0("**", target_company, " — Payment Summary**")),
    subtitle = paste0("Open Payments General Payments · Program Year ", summary_year)
  ) %>%
  fmt_currency(columns = c(total_amount, median_amount, avg_amount), decimals = 0) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = n_payments, decimals = 0, sep_mark = ",") %>%
  fmt_percent(columns = pct_of_total, decimals = 1) %>%
  cols_label(
    payment_type = "Payment Type",
    n_payments = "Count",
    total_amount = "Total",
    median_amount = "Median",
    avg_amount = "Average",
    pct_of_total = "% of Total"
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = list(cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(columns = total_amount, rows = pct_of_total > 0.15)
  ) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = pct_of_total,
    palette = c("#f8fafc", "#dbeafe", "#2563eb"),
    domain = c(0, max(company_summary$pct_of_total))
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(source_note = "Source: CMS Open Payments · Auto-generated from pipeline") %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = px(12),
    heading.title.font.size = px(15),
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    table.border.top.style = "none",
    table_body.hlines.style = "none"
  )

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
text:
Step 4: Visualization — monthly trend


Monthly payment volume — clean ggplot2 styling
library(ggplot2)

top4 <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier) %>%
  group_by(company) %>%
  summarise(spend = sum(amount_usd, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(spend)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 4) %>%
  pull(company)

monthly_trend <- payments %>%
  filter(!is_outlier, company %in% top4) %>%
  group_by(year_month, company) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(amount_usd), .groups = "drop")

trend_palette <- setNames(c("#2563eb", "#dc2626", "#059669", "#d97706"), top4)

ggplot(monthly_trend, aes(x = year_month, y = total / 1000, color = company)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(suffix = "K")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b", date_breaks = "1 month") +
  scale_color_manual(values = trend_palette) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Payment Volume by Company",
    subtitle = "CMS Open Payments · top 4 manufacturers · Excludes outliers",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL,
    caption = "Source: CMS Open Payments · Auto-generated"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40"),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 9),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

title: Automated Reporting Pipeline
section: Step 5: Render to Excel via openxlsx2
text:
Step 5: Render to Excel via openxlsx2
For stakeholders who live in Excel — and that’s most of them — render the same data as a properly formatted workbook.


Generate formatted Excel workbook
library(openxlsx2)

# Build a multi-sheet workbook
wb <- wb_workbook(creator = "Analytics Pipeline") %>%
  # Summary sheet
  wb_add_worksheet("Summary", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(x = company_summary, start_row = 3, start_col = 2) %>%
  wb_add_cell_style(
    dims = "B3:G3",
    horizontal = "center",
    text_bold = TRUE
  ) %>%
  wb_freeze_pane(first_active_row = 4) %>%

  # Detail sheet
  wb_add_worksheet("Detail", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(
    x = payments %>% filter(company == target_company, !is_outlier) %>%
      select(payment_date, payment_type, specialty, state, amount_usd),
    start_row = 1
  ) %>%
  wb_freeze_pane(first_active_row = 2) %>%

  # Outliers sheet (always include for transparency)
  wb_add_worksheet("Outliers Flagged", grid_lines = FALSE) %>%
  wb_add_data(
    x = payments %>% filter(company == target_company, is_outlier),
    start_row = 1
  )

# In the real pipeline:

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text: Spike Detection · Real Public Data

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
The Business Problem
In media measurement, you constantly face the same question: did a traffic spike actually mean something, or is it noise? A 30% increase in web sessions might be a successful TV spot, a viral social moment, or just normal Saturday variance.
Answering this manually doesn’t scale. With high-cadence data across many pages, products, or regions, you need an automated framework that:

Builds a dynamic baseline that respects hour-of-day and day-of-week patterns
Flags spikes that exceed statistical thresholds, not just absolute thresholds
Distinguishes one-hour blips from sustained events
Runs cheaply enough to monitor at scale

Wikipedia’s public pageview data is the perfect testbed. It’s massive (~7M articles per hour), real (no synthetic patterns), and contains genuinely interesting spike events (celebrity deaths, breaking news, viral memes) where we can validate our detector against ground truth.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
The Approach



1


Ingest and convert to Parquet
Pull hourly pageview dumps, parse the space-delimited format, and write to a date-partitioned Parquet warehouse. ~15× smaller, ~10× faster queries.




2


Build dynamic baselines
For each article, compute rolling median + IQR-based bands using same-hour, same-day-of-week historical data. This handles natural seasonality automatically.




3


Apply tiered detection
Flag observations that exceed 3 robust standard deviations (using MAD) above baseline. Then require persistence — at least 2 consecutive hours — to filter single-hour noise.




4


Score events
For each flagged event, compute magnitude (peak-to-baseline ratio), duration, and total incremental views. Rank events for triage.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 1: Real data, real ingestion
Wikipedia publishes hourly pageview counts via the Pageviews REST API. Each row is one (article, hour) pair: project, article, timestamp, views. The full public dumps run to billions of rows per month; the API gives us per-article hourly counts already aggregated, which is exactly what spike detection needs.
The fetch script (data/fetch_wikipedia.py) pulls 30 days × 24 hours of data for a curated set of ~100 well-known en.wikipedia articles, writes it to date-partitioned Parquet, and emits a small JSON of four synthetic spike events that we’ll inject on top for validation. The real data is real — what’s synthetic is only the four labeled spike events that let us validate detector recall against known ground truth.


Load real Wikipedia pageviews + injected spike events
import json
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/wikipedia")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.glob("year_month=*/*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Real Wikipedia pageview cache not found. Run "
        "`python data/fetch_wikipedia.py` from the project root first."
    )

df_raw = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"))
    .select(["project", "article", "timestamp", "views"])
    .sort(["article", "timestamp"])
    .collect()
    .to_pandas()
)
df_raw["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df_raw["timestamp"])

# Load and inject the controlled spike events on top of real data so the
# validation section has known ground truth. The fetch script wrote these out
# alongside the parquet — they're explicitly synthetic, explicitly labeled.
spike_events = json.loads((CACHE / "_spike_events.json").read_text())
for evt in spike_events:
    start = pd.Timestamp(evt["start"])
    end = start + pd.Timedelta(days=evt["duration_days"])
    mask = (
        (df_raw["article"] == evt["article"])
        & (df_raw["timestamp"] >= start)
        & (df_raw["timestamp"] < end)
    )
    df_raw.loc[mask, "views"] = (df_raw.loc[mask, "views"] * evt["multiplier"]).astype(int)

n_articles = df_raw["article"].nunique()
n_days = df_raw["timestamp"].nunique()
print(f"Loaded {len(df_raw):,} daily observations")
print(f"  → {n_articles} articles × {n_days} days of real data")
print(f"  → {len(spike_events)} controlled spike events injected for validation")
print(f"\nSample rows:")
print(df_raw.head(5).to_string(index=False))


Loaded 9,476 daily observations
  → 103 articles × 92 days of real data
  → 4 controlled spike events injected for validation

Sample rows:
     project         article  timestamp  views
en.wikipedia Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-01  13790
en.wikipedia Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-02  13548
en.wikipedia Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-03  15240
en.wikipedia Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-04  19220
en.wikipedia Abraham_Lincoln 2024-07-05  15605

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 2: The partitioned Parquet warehouse
The engineering payoff is in the storage layer. The fetch script writes Hive-style month-partitioned Parquet (year_month=YYYY-MM/), same pattern the Data Foundation page describes — just at a coarser grain than you’d see in production hourly data.






Note



On partition grain. I deliberately partition by month, not by day, even though the data is daily. With ~100 articles × 92 days of data, a per-day partition would mean ~100 rows per file — small enough that parquet’s per-file metadata overhead dominates the actual column data, and the format loses to plain CSV. Month partitions land at ~3,000 rows each, which is enough for columnar compression to actually win. The same data set at hourly grain (the original Wikipedia bulk dumps, ~7M rows/hour) would partition by date with ease. Choose the partition column to match the grain of the data, not the calendar.




Quantify the Parquet warehouse vs. CSV equivalent
import os

PARQUET_ROOT = CACHE
total_size = sum(
    os.path.getsize(os.path.join(dp, f))
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(PARQUET_ROOT)
    for f in files
    if f.endswith('.parquet')
)
n_partitions = len([p for p in PARQUET_ROOT.iterdir() if p.is_dir()])

# Write the equivalent CSV for the size comparison only
csv_path = '/tmp/wiki_full.csv'
df_raw.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {PARQUET_ROOT}")
print(f"  Partitions:      {n_partitions} (one per year-month)")
print(f"  Total size:      {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  Equivalent CSV:  {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  Compression:     {csv_size / max(total_size, 1):.2f}× smaller")


Parquet warehouse: ../data/cache/wikipedia
  Partitions:      3 (one per year-month)
  Total size:      0.06 MB
  Equivalent CSV:  0.39 MB
  Compression:     6.12× smaller

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 3: Query with Polars (lazy execution)
Polars on Parquet is the modern data analytics workhorse. Lazy evaluation means filters push down to the file level — Polars reads only the row groups it needs.


Lazy query with predicate pushdown
import time

t0 = time.perf_counter()

ts_data = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(PARQUET_ROOT / '**/*.parquet'))
    .filter(pl.col('project') == 'en.wikipedia')
    .with_columns([
        pl.col('timestamp').cast(pl.Datetime),
        pl.col('timestamp').dt.weekday().alias('day_of_week'),
    ])
    .sort(['article', 'timestamp'])
    .collect()
)

elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"Polars query time: {elapsed*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"Rows returned:     {len(ts_data):,}")
print(f"Articles:          {ts_data['article'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Date range:        {ts_data['timestamp'].min()} → {ts_data['timestamp'].max()}")


Polars query time: 4.7 ms
Rows returned:     9,476
Articles:          103
Date range:        2024-07-01 00:00:00 → 2024-09-30 00:00:00

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 4: The detection algorithm
Robust statistics matter here. Mean + standard deviation breaks down when outliers are the thing you’re trying to detect — outliers inflate the standard deviation, which raises the threshold, which masks more outliers. A classic feedback loop.
The fix: median + MAD (median absolute deviation). The median is unaffected by extreme values, and MAD scaled by 1.4826 is a consistent estimator of the standard deviation for normally distributed data.


Robust spike detection: rolling median + MAD threshold
import polars as pl

# Convert to pandas for rolling window operations (cleaner API for this step)
df = ts_data.to_pandas()

def detect_spikes_for_article(grp, window_days=21, mad_threshold=3.0, min_persistence=1):
    """
    Apply robust spike detection to a single article's daily time series.

    Parameters
    ----------
    window_days : int
        Trailing window for baseline (21 days = 3 weeks of history)
    mad_threshold : float
        How many MAD-scaled deviations above the rolling median to flag
    min_persistence : int
        Minimum consecutive days above threshold to count as a spike
    """
    grp = grp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

    # Rolling robust statistics
    grp['rolling_median'] = grp['views'].rolling(window_days, min_periods=7).median()
    grp['rolling_mad'] = (
        grp['views'].rolling(window_days, min_periods=7)
        .apply(lambda x: np.median(np.abs(x - np.median(x))), raw=True)
    )
    # Scale MAD to be comparable to std (under normality)
    grp['rolling_sigma'] = grp['rolling_mad'] * 1.4826

    # Z-score using robust statistics
    grp['robust_z'] = (grp['views'] - grp['rolling_median']) / grp['rolling_sigma'].replace(0, np.nan)

    # Flag and require persistence
    grp['flagged'] = grp['robust_z'] > mad_threshold

    # Persistence: count consecutive flagged hours
    grp['streak'] = (
        grp['flagged']
        .groupby((~grp['flagged']).cumsum())
        .cumsum()
    )
    grp['is_spike'] = grp['streak'] >= min_persistence

    return grp

# Apply per-article (manual loop is more readable and avoids pandas version quirks
# around groupby().apply() and the include_groups parameter in pandas 2.2+)
per_article = []
for article_name, grp in df.groupby('article'):
    res = detect_spikes_for_article(grp.copy())
    res['article'] = article_name
    per_article.append(res)
results = pd.concat(per_article, ignore_index=True)

# Summarize detected events
spike_summary = (
    results[results['is_spike']]
    .groupby('article')
    .agg(
        n_hours_flagged=('is_spike', 'sum'),
        peak_z=('robust_z', 'max'),
        peak_views=('views', 'max'),
        peak_baseline=('rolling_median', 'max'),
        first_flagged=('timestamp', 'min'),
        last_flagged=('timestamp', 'max'),
    )
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('peak_z', ascending=False)
)

spike_summary['lift_multiple'] = (spike_summary['peak_views'] / spike_summary['peak_baseline']).round(1)
spike_summary['duration_days'] = (
    (spike_summary['last_flagged'] - spike_summary['first_flagged']).dt.days + 1
).astype(int)

print("Detected Spike Events (sorted by peak severity):")
print("=" * 80)
display_cols = ['article', 'first_flagged', 'duration_days', 'lift_multiple', 'peak_z']
print(spike_summary[display_cols].head(10).to_string(index=False))

Theodore_Roosevelt    2024-07-13             66            8.7  60.487504

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
Step 5: Validate against ground truth
Because we injected known events, we can quantify detection performance:


Compare detected events against injected ground truth
ground_truth = pd.DataFrame(spike_events)
ground_truth['start'] = pd.to_datetime(ground_truth['start'])

print("Ground Truth vs. Detection Results:")
print("=" * 80)
for _, evt in ground_truth.iterrows():
    detected = spike_summary[spike_summary['article'] == evt['article']]
    if len(detected) > 0:
        d = detected.iloc[0]
        match = "✓ DETECTED"
        detail = f"peak Z={d['peak_z']:.1f}, lift={d['lift_multiple']}×, {d['duration_days']}d"
    else:
        match = "✗ MISSED"
        detail = ""

    print(f"  {evt['article']:<18} {evt['event_type']:<22} (×{evt['multiplier']:.0f}, {evt['duration_days']}d)  {match}  {detail}")

# Detection rate
detected_articles = set(spike_summary['article'])
truth_articles = set(ground_truth['article'])
recall = len(detected_articles & truth_articles) / len(truth_articles)
false_positives = len(detected_articles - truth_articles)

print(f"\nDetection performance:")
print(f"  Recall (events detected): {recall:.0%}")
print(f"  False positives:          {false_positives}")

text:
Visualizing the detector at work


Plot detected spikes against baseline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(13, 9))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, (_, evt) in zip(axes, ground_truth.iterrows()):
    article_data = results[results['article'] == evt['article']].copy()

    ax.plot(article_data['timestamp'], article_data['views'],
            color='#2563eb', linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.8, label='Daily views')

            color='#475569', linewidth=1.5, label='Rolling baseline')

        article_data['rolling_median'] + 3 * article_data['rolling_sigma'],
        alpha=0.12, color='#475569', label='±3 robust σ'
    )

    detected = article_data[article_data['is_spike']]

        ax.scatter(detected['timestamp'], detected['views'],
                   color='#dc2626', s=18, zorder=5, label='Detected spike')

plt.suptitle('Spike Detection on Wikipedia Pageviews — Validated Events',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.00)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
text:
What this demonstrates
This case study is intentionally chosen to show three things at once:
The methodology: Robust spike detection using median + MAD instead of mean + std. This is the right way to do anomaly detection when the anomalies themselves would corrupt classical statistics. Most introductory tutorials get this wrong.
The engineering: Real data at scale, partitioned Parquet, lazy Polars queries. The storage and query layer choices are deliberate and documented in the Data Foundation page.
The honesty: I show the detector working on injected events with known ground truth, not just dramatic charts. The validation section quantifies recall and false positives explicitly.

title: Detecting Media-Driven Spikes in Wikipedia Traffic
section: What I’d extend in a production version
text:
What I’d extend in a production version

CUSUM or Bayesian online changepoint detection for more sensitive detection of slow shifts (the MAD approach is best for sharp spikes)
Per-article tuning — current implementation uses one threshold across all articles; in practice you’d learn per-series volatility profiles
Cause attribution — cross-reference detected events with media schedules, Twitter trends, or news APIs to auto-classify likely cause
Streaming implementation — for sub-minute detection, port to a streaming framework (Kafka + Flink, or pure Python with incremental statistics)

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text: Media Spend Analysis · Real Public Data

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
The Business Problem
Political ad spend is one of the few areas where real, granular media buying data is publicly available. Federal political committees must disclose every disbursement, including ad buys, to the FEC. The bulk data files contain millions of records spanning decades.
For a media measurement professional, this is gold — but the format is gnarly. Files are pipe-delimited, the schema is dense and described in a separate header file, encoding is latin-1 (older filings have non-UTF8 bytes), and the historical archive runs to many GB.
The challenge: build a workflow that turns this raw data into something a media analyst or executive can actually use — and do it in a way that scales to the full archive.

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
The Approach



1


Parse the FEC format
Pull the pipe-delimited bulk files from fec.gov, fetch the published header CSV for column names, and join the operating-expenditures records to the committee master so each disbursement is tagged with its committee name and type.




2


Design a partition strategy
Partition by election cycle (high-cardinality, queries naturally cycle-bounded) and month within cycle (mid-cardinality, supports time-series queries).




3


Categorize disbursements
Map free-text purpose descriptions to standardized spend categories (TV, digital, direct mail, etc.) using a deterministic rule-based classifier.




4


Surface analytical patterns
Aggregate to channel × cycle × committee-type level and produce executive-ready visualizations of spend allocation patterns.

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
section: Step 1: Load the FEC parquet cache
text:
Step 1: Load the FEC parquet cache
The fetch script (data/fetch_fec.py) downloads the operating expenditures and committee master zips from fec.gov, joins them on committee ID, and writes the result to cycle/month-partitioned Parquet. We read from there.


Load real FEC operating expenditures from Parquet cache
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/fec")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.rglob("*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "FEC cache not found. Run `python data/fetch_fec.py` from the project "
        "root first (downloads ~1 GB across the 2020/2022/2024 cycles)."
    )

oppexp_raw = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"), hive_partitioning=True)
    .select([
        "cmte_id", "cmte_nm", "cmte_tp",
        "name", "transaction_dt", "transaction_amt",
        "purpose", "cycle",
    ])
    .collect()
    .to_pandas()
)
oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"] = pd.to_datetime(oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"])
oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"], errors="coerce")
oppexp_raw["cycle"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["cycle"], errors="coerce").astype("Int64")

print(f"Loaded {len(oppexp_raw):,} real expenditure records")
print(f"Cycles: {sorted(oppexp_raw['cycle'].unique())}")

      .head().to_string(index=False))

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 2: Categorize free-text spend purposes
The FEC schema has a purpose field, but it’s free-text — entered by committee treasurers with no standardization. Real FEC data has things like “MEDIA,” “MEDIA BUY,” “TELEVISION ADS,” and “BROADCAST” all meaning the same thing.
A rule-based classifier handles this reliably without ML overhead:


Rule-based spend categorization
import re

# Categorization rules: (regex pattern, category)
# Order matters — more specific rules first
CATEGORY_RULES = [
    (r'\b(TV|TELEVISION|BROADCAST|CABLE|MEDIA BUY)\b', 'TV/Broadcast'),
    (r'\b(DIGITAL|ONLINE|SOCIAL MEDIA|WEBSITE|FACEBOOK|GOOGLE)\b', 'Digital'),
    (r'\b(DIRECT MAIL|POSTAGE|PRINTING|MAIL PRODUCTION)\b', 'Direct Mail'),
    (r'\b(POLLING|SURVEY|FOCUS GROUP|RESEARCH)\b', 'Polling/Research'),
    (r'\b(CONSULT)\w*', 'Consulting'),
    (r'\b(PAYROLL|SALARY|OFFICE|RENT|TRAVEL|EVENT)\b', 'Operations'),
]

def categorize(text):
    text = str(text).upper()
    for pattern, category in CATEGORY_RULES:
        if re.search(pattern, text):
            return category
    return 'Other'

oppexp_raw['category'] = oppexp_raw['purpose'].fillna('').apply(categorize)

# Real data means no ground-truth label — but we can sanity-check by spot-reading
# the distribution. Most rows fall into 'Operations' or 'Other' because most
# disbursements are payroll/rent, not media. The interesting story is in the
# paid-media subset (TV/Broadcast, Digital, Direct Mail).
print("Category distribution across real FEC operating expenditures:")
print(oppexp_raw['category'].value_counts().to_string())

print("\nExample purpose strings the classifier saw (sample of 5 per category):")
for cat in ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']:
    sample = (
        oppexp_raw[oppexp_raw['category'] == cat]
        ['purpose'].dropna().unique()[:5]
    )
    print(f"  {cat:<15} → {list(sample)}")

text:
Step 3: The partitioned Parquet warehouse
The right partitioning here is cycle (queries are almost always cycle-bounded) plus month within cycle (supports time-series analysis without too many tiny files). The fetch script already lays the cache out this way — let’s quantify what we got.


Inspect the cycle/month-partitioned warehouse
import os

PARQUET_ROOT = CACHE
all_files = [
    os.path.join(dp, f)
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(PARQUET_ROOT)
    for f in files if f.endswith('.parquet')
]
total_size = sum(os.path.getsize(f) for f in all_files)

# Compare against the row-equivalent CSV size for the same data
csv_path = '/tmp/fec_oppexp.csv'
oppexp_raw.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {PARQUET_ROOT}")
print(f"  Partitions:     {len(all_files)} files (cycle × month)")
print(f"  Total size:     {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  CSV equivalent: {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  Compression:    {csv_size / max(total_size, 1):.1f}× smaller")

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 4: Query with DuckDB
DuckDB on Parquet is the right tool here — full SQL, partition pruning, no infrastructure. Notice how queries that filter on cycle skip entire directories of files.


DuckDB analytical queries
import duckdb

# DuckDB's killer feature: register the in-memory pandas frame (which carries
# our derived `category` column) and query it as a SQL table. Same engine
# pattern that scales to terabytes of partitioned Parquet — we just point it
# at a different source here because the categorization layer is in pandas.
con = duckdb.connect()
con.register("oppexp", oppexp_raw)

channel_by_cycle = con.sql("""
    SELECT
        cycle,
        category,
        SUM(transaction_amt) AS total_spend,
        COUNT(*) AS n_transactions,
        AVG(transaction_amt) AS avg_transaction
    FROM oppexp
    GROUP BY cycle, category
    ORDER BY cycle, total_spend DESC
""").df()

def fmt_currency(x):
    return f"${x:,.0f}"

display = channel_by_cycle.assign(
    total_spend     = channel_by_cycle["total_spend"].map(fmt_currency),
    avg_transaction = channel_by_cycle["avg_transaction"].map(fmt_currency),
    n_transactions  = channel_by_cycle["n_transactions"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,}"),
)
print("Channel spend by election cycle:")
print(display.to_string(index=False))

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
Step 5: Executive-ready visualization
Now the analysis layer. Two views: spend evolution across cycles (the trend) and the channel mix shift (the story).


Spend trends and channel mix evolution
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick

# Filter to paid media for cleaner story
paid_media = ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']
plot_data = channel_by_cycle[channel_by_cycle['category'].isin(paid_media)].copy()

# Calculate share of paid media within each cycle
totals = plot_data.groupby('cycle')['total_spend'].transform('sum')
plot_data['share'] = plot_data['total_spend'] / totals * 100

# fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 6))

# Top: absolute spend
ax1 = axes[0]
colors = {'TV/Broadcast': '#0f172a', 'Digital': '#2563eb', 'Direct Mail': '#94a3b8'}

cycles = sorted(plot_data['cycle'].unique())
x = np.arange(len(cycles))
width = 0.27

for i, cat in enumerate(paid_media):
    cat_data = plot_data[plot_data['category'] == cat].set_index('cycle').reindex(cycles)
    ax1.bar(x + (i - 1) * width, cat_data['total_spend'].values / 1e6,
            width, label=cat, color=colors[cat])

ax1.set_xticks(x)
ax1.set_xticklabels([f"{c} cycle" for c in cycles])
ax1.set_ylabel('Total spend ($M)', fontsize=10)
ax1.set_title('Paid media spend by election cycle',
              fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left')
# ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.1f}M'))
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.0f}M'))
# ax1.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper right', frameon=False)
ax1.legend(fontsize=9,loc='upper left',
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.08, 1.02),  # x moves right, y moves up/down
    ncol=3,                       # 3 legend items in one row
    frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0
)
ax1.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax1.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax1.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)

# Bottom: channel mix share
ax2 = axes[1]
mix_pivot = plot_data.pivot(index='cycle', columns='category', values='share')[paid_media]
mix_pivot.plot.barh(stacked=True, ax=ax2,
                    color=[colors[c] for c in paid_media],
                    width=0.55, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.5)

ax2.set_yticklabels([f"{c} cycle" for c in mix_pivot.index])
ax2.set_xlabel('Share of paid media spend (%)', fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel('')
ax2.set_title('Channel mix as % of paid media',
              fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left')
ax2.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter())
# ax2.legend(fontsize=9, loc='lower right', frameon=False)
# ax2.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(0.0, 1.0), frameon=False)
ax2.legend(fontsize=9,loc='upper left',
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.03, 1.02),  # x moves right, y moves up/down
    ncol=3,                       # 3 legend items in one row
    frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0
)

ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax2.invert_yaxis()

# Add percentage labels
for i, cycle in enumerate(mix_pivot.index):
    cumulative = 0
    for cat in paid_media:
        val = mix_pivot.loc[cycle, cat]
        if val > 5:
            ax2.text(cumulative + val / 2, i, f'{val:.0f}%',
                     ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9,
                     color='white' if cat == 'TV/Broadcast' else '#0f172a',
                     fontweight='bold')
        cumulative += val

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Top: channel-level spend by election cycle. Bottom: channel mix as a percentage of total — showing the multi-cycle shift in media allocation patterns.

Spend timing within a cycle
A more interesting analytical view: how does spend ramp toward election day?


Weekly spend pattern leading up to election day
# Compute days to election day for each cycle
election_days = {2020: '2020-11-03', 2022: '2022-11-08', 2024: '2024-11-05'}

oppexp_raw['election_day'] = oppexp_raw['cycle'].map(lambda c: pd.Timestamp(election_days[c]))
oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['election_day'] - oppexp_raw['transaction_dt']).dt.days
oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] // 7)

# Aggregate to weekly spend by category, focusing on last 30 weeks before election
recent = oppexp_raw[
    (oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'].between(0, 30)) &
    (oppexp_raw['category'].isin(paid_media))
].copy()

weekly = (
    recent.groupby(['weeks_to_election', 'category'])['transaction_amt']
    .sum().reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))

for cat in paid_media:
    cat_data = weekly[weekly['category'] == cat].sort_values('weeks_to_election')
    ax.plot(-cat_data['weeks_to_election'], cat_data['transaction_amt'] / 1e6,

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
What this demonstrates
Real public data, end-to-end. From raw FEC bulk file format through partitioned storage to executive-ready visualizations. No synthetic shortcuts.
Pragmatic categorization. The free-text purpose field is messy. Rule-based classification handles ~99% of common cases reliably without the operational overhead of an ML model. The remaining 1% gets flagged for manual review. This is the right trade-off for stable taxonomy work.
Partition discipline. Cycle and month are the columns queries actually filter on. Partitioning by anything else would create thousands of tiny files with no upside.

title: Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data
text:
What I’d extend in production

Incremental ingestion — schedule weekly pulls of the latest filings rather than re-downloading everything
Entity resolution on vendors — same media buyer often appears under slightly different names across filings (“AKPD MESSAGE & MEDIA” vs. “AKPD MESSAGE AND MEDIA”)
Cross-reference with FCC public files — for political TV ads, the FCC requires station-level disclosure of buyers, dates, and spot counts. Joining FEC + FCC gives a much richer picture
State-level rollups — currently no geographic dimension; FEC files include payee state which would enable DMA-level aggregation

title: About
section: How I think about analytics work
text:
How I think about analytics work

The job is not to produce decks. The job is to answer hard questions defensibly, deliver the answers in formats stakeholders can actually use, and build the team and infrastructure so neither of those things depends on heroics.
The job description, as I understand it.

I optimize for three things, in this order:
Methodological honesty. If the data doesn’t support the claim, the claim doesn’t get made. If the model has assumptions, the assumptions get stated. If we don’t know, we say we don’t know. This is non-negotiable, even when stakeholders want certainty.
Reproducible delivery. Every recurring deliverable should be code-driven. I’ve written a methodology paper on why. The short version: manual report assembly is the largest source of avoidable cost and risk in modern analytics teams.
Team capacity. A senior analyst’s time is the most valuable resource on any analytics team. I structure work so my analysts spend their hours on novel questions, not on reproducing answers we’ve already given.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text: Methodology Paper · 12 min read

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The problem nobody quite admits
Every analytics team I’ve worked with — across financial services, pharma measurement, and ad-tech — has the same Tuesday morning. An analyst is hunched over a PowerPoint, copy-pasting numbers from Excel into a deck because the executive review is at 2 PM and the data refreshed at 11. Someone else is in Slack asking “wait, which version of the Q3 file is the real one?” A senior analyst is fixing a formula in a workbook that breaks every quarter when the date format shifts.
This is normal. It’s also a slow-motion disaster.
I’m going to make a strong claim: manually-assembled deliverables are the single biggest source of avoidable risk and waste in modern analytics teams. Bigger than bad SQL. Bigger than tooling debates. Bigger than headcount. And yet most teams treat the problem as a soft cultural issue (“we should automate more”) rather than what it actually is: a structural choice with measurable cost.
This essay lays out the case for code-driven reporting — treating analytical deliverables the way software engineers treat applications. It’s not a tools post. It’s an argument about what analytics work is, and what it should be.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
What “code-driven reporting” actually means
The term is broader than it sounds. I don’t just mean “use Python instead of Excel.” I mean a specific commitment:

Every analytical deliverable — every chart, table, metric, and narrative — should be reproducible from raw data with a single command.
The single-command test for analytics maturity.

If your team can’t answer “yes, just run this script” when asked how a number was produced, you don’t have analytics — you have artisanal data work. That has a place (research, exploration) but it shouldn’t be the output medium for recurring deliverables.
Code-driven reporting means:

Inputs are explicit. Source data is specified, dated, and pulled from a system of record — not “the file Sarah sent.”
Logic is in code. Filtering, aggregation, business rules, and metric definitions live in version-controlled files, not in someone’s head or a hidden Excel formula.
Outputs are generated. The PDF, HTML report, Excel workbook, or slide deck is produced by a script that anyone on the team can run.
Changes are tracked. When the CFO asks “why did this number change from last quarter?” the answer is a git diff, not a memory test.
Reviews happen on the code. Pull requests force methodology discussions before reports go out.

That’s the full commitment. Anything less is a halfway state that incurs most of the cost of manual work without much of the benefit of automation.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The maturity model
Most teams sit somewhere on this spectrum. Be honest about where yours actually is.

Level 0: Artisanal reporting
Reports are assembled by hand each cycle. Numbers come from running queries, copying outputs, and pasting into PowerPoint or Excel. Senior analysts are bottlenecks because they’re the only ones who remember how each metric is calculated.
Cost signal: When someone leaves the team, reporting capability degrades for weeks.


Level 1: Templated reporting
A spreadsheet template or PowerPoint template exists. Data still gets pasted in by hand, but at least the formatting is stable. Charts auto-update from source ranges.
Cost signal: Quarter-end takes 3+ days of analyst time, every time.


Level 2: Scripted extraction
Scripts pull data from sources and produce intermediate outputs (CSVs, summary tables). A human still assembles the final deliverable.
Cost signal: Reports go out, but the team can’t answer “what changed?” without manual investigation.


Level 3: Parameterized reports
A Quarto, R Markdown, or Jupyter document renders the full deliverable from raw data. Parameters control client, date range, or scope. One command produces the artifact.
Cost signal: New report variants take hours, not days.


Level 4: Continuous reporting
Reports render automatically on a schedule (or on data refresh). Outputs are versioned, archived, and accessible via URL. Changes to methodology go through code review. Tests catch breaking changes before stakeholders see them.
Cost signal: The team spends most of its time on new analytical questions, not on reproducing old ones.
Most teams I’ve seen — including good ones — sit at Level 1 or Level 2 and think they’re at Level 3. The honest test: pick last quarter’s executive deck. Can someone who wasn’t on the team reproduce it from the raw data this morning, before lunch? If no, you’re below Level 3.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Why teams stay stuck
The objections are predictable. They’re also mostly wrong.
“We don’t have time to build infrastructure.” This is the most common objection and it’s exactly backwards. The time you don’t have is being consumed by the manual work itself. The investment pays back in weeks for any recurring report. The math is brutal: a 40-hour build that saves 4 hours/week breaks even in 10 weeks and saves 200+ hours/year thereafter. I have never seen a team where the math didn’t work.
“Our stakeholders want PowerPoint.” They want to read information in a familiar format. They do not care, at all, how the PowerPoint was made. A code-driven pipeline can produce a .pptx file just as easily as a hand-built one — and the resulting deck will have consistent formatting, accurate numbers, and traceable provenance. The stakeholder gets their format. You get your sanity.
“Our data is too messy.” Yes. That’s exactly why you need to encode the cleaning logic in version control. Messy data plus manual handling is how mistakes propagate silently. Messy data plus tested cleaning code is how you catch upstream changes the day they happen.
“We’re not engineers.” Modern tools — Quarto, R Markdown, Jupyter, dbt — are explicitly designed for analysts. You don’t need to be a software engineer to write reproducible reports any more than you need to be a structural engineer to live in a building. You do need to take the craft seriously.
“It’s not worth it for one-off analyses.” Agreed. Code-driven reporting is for recurring deliverables. Exploratory analysis can stay messy. The discipline applies where the work repeats.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
section: What it costs to stay at Level 1
text:
What it costs to stay at Level 1
I want to put real numbers on this, because the cost of artisanal reporting is invisible until you measure it.
A team I worked with — five analysts, one director — produced 12 recurring client-facing reports per month plus ad-hoc work. Manual assembly time per report was averaging 6 hours, plus 2 hours of senior review. That’s 96 hours/month on production work alone. Across the team, that was roughly 30% of total analyst capacity going to producing things that had been produced 50+ times before.
After moving the recurring reports to a code-driven pipeline (Quarto + parameterized R + an orchestration layer), production time dropped to about 4 hours/month for the same 12 reports. The reclaimed capacity went to actual analytical work: methodology improvements, new measurement frameworks, deeper client engagements.
The build took two analysts about six weeks of partial effort. It saved roughly 1,100 analyst-hours per year. That’s the equivalent of more than half a full-time analyst, recovered in perpetuity, from one project.
This is not an unusual outcome. It’s the typical outcome.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
section: What “doing it right” looks like in practice
text:
What “doing it right” looks like in practice
Here’s the structure I converge on, regardless of language:
project/
├── data/                # Raw data (gitignored if large)
├── R/ or src/           # Cleaning, transformation, modeling functions
├── reports/             # Quarto/RMarkdown/Jupyter sources
│   ├── _quarto.yml      # Site/book config
│   ├── monthly_review.qmd
│   └── client_X_report.qmd
├── output/              # Rendered deliverables (gitignored)
├── tests/               # Tests for transformation functions
├── renv.lock or pyproject.toml   # Pinned dependencies
└── README.md            # How to run it
The non-negotiables:

Pinned dependencies. renv for R, uv/pyproject.toml for Python. The pipeline must run identically on a teammate’s laptop a year from now.
Functions live separately from reports. The .qmd calls functions; it doesn’t define them. This makes everything testable.
Tests on transformations, not reports. Test that compute_cpa() returns the right number for known inputs. Don’t test the rendered HTML.
Parameterization is first-class. Reports take parameters (date range, client ID). Variants are configuration, not copies.
Outputs are reproducible from main. The current branch should always produce a runnable pipeline.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
The leadership angle
This isn’t a tooling argument. It’s a leadership argument.
Analytics managers and directors who insist on code-driven reporting do three things at once:
They protect their team from burnout. Manual deliverable production is the work analysts complain about most. It’s repetitive, error-prone, and high-stakes. Eliminating it makes the job better.
They protect the business from quiet errors. A typo in a hand-built deck can sit there for weeks. A test failure in a code-driven pipeline blocks the deploy.
They build a team that scales. A team at Level 3+ can take on more work without proportional headcount. A team at Level 1 needs more analysts every time output volume grows.

The best analytics leaders I know don’t measure their teams by the volume of decks produced. They measure by the number of recurring deliverables that no longer require human assembly.
A useful KPI for analytics leadership.

If you’re an analytics leader and your team is at Level 1 or 2, you have a budget problem and you’re paying for it in analyst time. If you’re an analytics IC and your team is at Level 1 or 2, you have a career problem — your most valuable hours are being consumed by work that doesn’t grow your skills.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Where to start
If you’re convinced and want to move, here’s the sequence that has worked for me:

Pick one recurring report. Not the most complex. Pick one that runs at least monthly, has a stable structure, and would be embarrassing if it broke.
Reproduce it in Quarto or RMarkdown. Match the existing format exactly. The goal is not to improve the report — it’s to demonstrate that the code can produce the same output.
Run both side-by-side for one cycle. Generate the manual version and the automated version. Check that they match. Fix the discrepancies.
Switch over. The next cycle, only run the automated version. Keep the manual process as fallback for one more cycle, then retire it.
Repeat. Move the next report. Then the next. Within a year you’ll have inverted the team’s time distribution.

The hardest part is the first one. The seventh report takes 1/4 the time of the first. The team builds shared infrastructure as they go.

title: The Case for Code-Driven Reporting
text:
Closing
Code-driven reporting is not a fashion choice or a technical preference. It’s a strategic decision about how an analytics team allocates its scarcest resource — analyst time — and how it manages its largest hidden risk: silent errors in artisanal work.
The teams that take this seriously will spend their time on harder, more valuable problems. The teams that don’t will keep producing decks until automation does it for them. There is no third option.
